patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old woman with sickle cell disease. You are anxious but speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve got this really bad pain in my chest and upper back that won’t let up.” Volunteer: deep aching chest/upper back pain since last night, worse than prior crises; short of breath walking to the bathroom; home pain meds aren’t helping; you’ve had crises before.
If asked, add: worse with deep breaths; low-grade fever/chills since this morning; mild mostly dry cough; very tired and a bit lightheaded standing; darker urine; poor water intake.
Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about risks/meds: long car ride 2 days ago without stretching; ran out of folic acid; loud snoring/night gasping; legs swelling by evening.
Be resistant/hesitant if asked about substances or adherence: admit smoking/vaping but ask to keep from family; hesitate then admit cocaine a week ago; admit not taking hydroxyurea due to stomach upset and fear.
Express misconceptions: worry it’s a heart attack; mention months of exertional chest tightness; worry a recent “cold” became pneumonia.
If clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, share more, and ask about costs/appointments and fear losing voxelotor or surprise copays.

medications: Voxelotor 1500 mg PO once daily
Folic acid 1 mg PO once daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever
Oxycodone 5 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for severe pain
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/inhalation: 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed for wheeze
confounders: Fever and leukocytosis can reflect infection vs inflammatory response during vaso-occlusive crisis
Asthma with chest symptoms can mimic or coexist with acute chest syndrome
Microalbuminuria/proteinuria can be mistaken for UTI or primary renal disease
History of gallstones can cause RUQ pain and elevated bilirubin, confounding hemolysis-related jaundice
Possible dehydration (mild hyponatremia, low bicarbonate) can worsen pain crisis and mimic sepsis physiology
